figures.gloss: The semi-divine sage who remembered his past lives, could allegedly bilocate, and heard the music of the spheres. He established a secretive, ascetic brotherhood dedicated to purifying the immortal soul through mathematics, music, and ritual silence.
figures.facet: He bridges the ecstatic mystic and the rational geometer, making mathematics a salvific, spiritual discipline where theorems are not utilitarian tools, but direct revelations of the divine mind.
figures.provenance: Iamblichus, 'Life of Pythagoras'; Porphyry; Diogenes Laertius; Heraclitus (fragments).

figures.gloss: A sacred triangular figure consisting of ten points arranged in four descending rows (1, 2, 3, and 4). It served as the brotherhood's supreme object of veneration, containing the fundamental musical concords and the spatial dimensions.
figures.facet: It divinizes pure arithmetic. It collapses the entirety of existence—from point, to line, to surface, to solid—into a perfect, elegant numerical progression that rules the cosmos.
figures.provenance: Aetius; Sextus Empiricus, 'Against the Mathematicians'; Iamblichus; pseudo-Plutarch.

figures.gloss: The doctrine that the immortal soul is a fallen divine entity trapped in a cycle of reincarnation, capable of migrating across species boundaries into human, animal, or even plant bodies based on past actions.
figures.facet: It violently erases the absolute ontological barrier between human and animal life prevalent in Greek thought, making dietary taboos like vegetarianism an urgent moral necessity to avoid cannibalizing one's kin.

figures.gloss: The foundational ontological pairs of the universe: the Limit (the Monad, odd, male, light, good) and the Unlimited (the Dyad, even, female, dark, bad). All visible reality is generated by the tension between these opposing columns.
figures.facet: It establishes a highly structured, mathematical dualism where moral and physical goodness is equated explicitly with precise geometric limitation, while evil is equated with boundless, chaotic infinity.

figures.gloss: The theory that the sun, moon, and planets emit unique orbital hums based on their mathematical proportions. Together, they produce a perfect cosmic symphony that ordinary humans are too deafened by habit to hear.
figures.facet: It translates acoustic ratios discovered on a monochord to the scale of the heavens, literalizing the idea that the universe is not just ordered, but actively, physically singing.
figures.provenance: Aristotle, 'On the Heavens'; Pliny the Elder; Ptolemy, 'Harmonics'.

figures.gloss: The 'things heard'—rigid, cryptic oral maxims governing daily life. They included strict prohibitions such as 'do not eat beans,' 'do not stir the fire with a knife,' and 'do not look in a mirror beside a lamp.'
figures.facet: These seemingly arbitrary taboos fuse deep primitive superstition with philosophical symbolism, creating an all-encompassing way of life where every mundane action is fraught with cosmic risk.

figures.gloss: The cosmic hearth or 'Watchtower of Zeus' around which the Earth, Sun, Moon, and planets orbit. Formulated by Philolaus, this model posits an invisible 'Counter-Earth' to bring the number of celestial bodies to the perfect ten.
figures.facet: It radically displaces the Earth from the center of the universe centuries before Copernicus, driven not by empirical astronomy but by the absolute, religious necessity of mathematical perfection.

figures.gloss: A 5th-century BCE Pythagorean (of Croton, later Thebes) whose book — the first work by any member of the school to break its vow of secrecy — is the surviving source for the Limit/Unlimited ontology and the Central Fire cosmology already credited to him in this roster's own entries. Only fragments survive, transmitted through later doxography.
figures.facet: He is the hinge between oral mystery-cult and written philosophy — the moment Pythagorean doctrine left the closed circle of initiates and entered the wider Greek intellectual record.
figures.provenance: Philolaus, fragments (Diels-Kranz 44), preserved via Stobaeus and other doxographers; Carl Huffman, Philolaus of Croton: Pythagorean and Presocratic (1993).

figures.gloss: A 4th-century BCE Pythagorean who was repeatedly elected strategos (general) of Tarentum while advancing mathematics and mechanics; his mechanical solution to the 'doubling of the cube' and his work on musical proportion are among the school's most historically verifiable scientific achievements. He corresponded with Plato and, by some ancient accounts, helped secure Plato's safety from Dionysius II of Syracuse.
figures.facet: He is the counter-evidence to Pythagoreanism's mystical reputation — proof the school also produced a working mathematician and elected head of state, briefly realizing the philosopher-statesman Plato theorized.
figures.provenance: Diogenes Laertius, Lives of the Eminent Philosophers, Book VIII; fragments preserved by Porphyry and Iamblichus; Carl Huffman, Archytas of Tarentum: Pythagorean, Philosopher and Mathematician King (2005).

figures.gloss: A Hellenistic-era compilation of Pythagorean ethical precepts in hexameter verse, traditionally but not literally ascribed to Pythagoras, prescribing reverence for the gods, filial duty, and a nightly self-examination — 'Where did I transgress? What did I do? What duty's left undone?' — before sleep. It closes with the promise that the purified soul, freed from the body, becomes a deathless god.
figures.facet: It converts the school's oral akousmata into a portable, memorizable catechism — the form through which Pythagorean ethics traveled long after the historical brotherhood dissolved, reaching its widest late-antique audience through Hierocles' 5th-century-CE commentary.
figures.provenance: Carmen Aureum (Greek text, uncertain Hellenistic compiler); Hierocles of Alexandria, Commentary on the Golden Verses (5th c. CE).

figures.gloss: The two self-identified branches of the Pythagorean brotherhood reported by Iamblichus: the akousmatikoi ('hearers'), who preserved Pythagoras's maxims as unquestioned oral taboo without seeking their reasons, and the mathematikoi ('learners'), who pursued the demonstrative mathematical science later ages associate with the school's name. Ancient sources report each faction denied the other was truly Pythagorean.
figures.facet: It exposes that 'Pythagoreanism' was never one coherent doctrine even in antiquity but a contested inheritance — the same brotherhood producing both the maxim-observant conservatives and the mathematicians whose cosmology reads as a wholly different intellectual project.
figures.provenance: Iamblichus, On the Pythagorean Life (De Vita Pythagorica), ch. 18; Walter Burkert, Lore and Science in Ancient Pythagoreanism (1972).

figures.gloss: An early Pythagorean (early-to-mid 5th century BCE) associated in antiquity with disclosing either the existence of irrational magnitudes (incommensurability) or the construction of the dodecahedron — knowledge the brotherhood reportedly wished kept secret. A late legendary tradition holds the sea took him, drowned as divine punishment for the disclosure; the drowning story is a moralizing legend, not a historical account, and modern scholarship doubts its details.
figures.facet: He is the school's own myth of forbidden knowledge — proof that Pythagoreanism could treat mathematical discovery itself as capable of sacrilege, a boundary no other Greek philosophical school drew around geometry.
figures.provenance: Iamblichus, On the Pythagorean Life 247; Pappus of Alexandria's commentary on Euclid Book X (drowning legend); Walter Burkert, Lore and Science in Ancient Pythagoreanism.

figures.gloss: Reputed wife (in some accounts daughter or student) of Pythagoras and the best-known of the school's documented women members — Iamblichus's catalogue names seventeen women among the initiates. Letters and maxims circulating under her name (on household management, child-rearing, and moderation) are pseudepigraphic, composed centuries after any historical Theano could have lived.
figures.facet: She is the evidentiary trace of an otherwise undocumented reality — that this mystery-school admitted women to philosophical membership — filtered entirely through later, invented texts that make her legend larger than anything verifiable about the woman herself.
figures.provenance: Iamblichus, On the Pythagorean Life, ch. 36 (catalogue of members); pseudo-Theano letters (Hellenistic-Roman period, pseudepigraphic); Sarah Pomeroy, Pythagorean Women: Their History and Writings (2013).

edges.note: Intra-tradition: The enlightened mystic and the supreme, absolute geometric structure he revealed to his initiated disciples.
Intra-tradition: The metaphysical threat of rebirth and the daily, obsessive ritual purity required to safeguard the soul against it.
Cross-tradition GOLD SEAM: The cyclical trap of repeated births where moral impurity guarantees continued material imprisonment.
Cross-tradition GOLD SEAM: The radical extension of kinship to all life forms, making strict vegetarianism an absolute necessity to avoid harming migrating souls.
Cross-tradition GOLD SEAM: The generation of all reality through the tension and balance of a primordial binary of light/dark, male/female, odd/even.
Cross-tradition GOLD SEAM: Explicitly adopted by later Gnostics and Neoplatonists who took Pythagorean arithmetic as the literal blueprint for divine emanation.
Cross-tradition GOLD SEAM: The Hermetic axiom directly absorbed Pythagorean mathematical harmonics as the mechanism binding the celestial and terrestrial planes.
Cross-tradition GOLD SEAM: The silent, perfect, underlying frequency and rhythm that governs the movements of heaven and earth.
Cross-tradition GOLD SEAM: Rigid, highly cryptic behavioral prescriptions that translate immense cosmic anxieties into daily, hyper-specific taboos.
Cross-tradition GOLD SEAM: Semi-divine sages who shape reality not through brute force, but through acoustic perfection, song, and esoteric knowledge.
Cross-tradition GOLD SEAM: Conflated in late antiquity as supreme esoteric masters who delivered both mathematical science and spiritual salvation.
Cross-tradition GOLD SEAM: The concentrated, infinitesimal geometric seed that contains the structural blueprint for all dimensions of the universe.
Cross-tradition GOLD SEAM: The cosmos understood not as a political kingdom of fickle gods, but as a rigid, inherently mathematical structure.
Cross-tradition GOLD SEAM: The invisible hearth-fire anchoring the universe, around which all celestial bodies perform their mathematical dance.
Cross-tradition GOLD SEAM: The ontological pairing of an active, limiting, ordering principle against a boundless, passive material substrate.
Cross-tradition GOLD SEAM: The cosmic order conceived as perfect proportion and balance, equally applicable to acoustic tuning and moral justice.
Herodotus (2.81) already links Orphic and Pythagorean burial and dietary rules as the same ascetic complex; ancient sources treat the two regimens as close kin, not independent parallels.
ancient sources (Herodotus, Diogenes Laertius) closely associate Orphic and Pythagorean reincarnation doctrine, though which direction the borrowing ran is unresolved.
Intra-tradition: Philolaus is the documented author of the Central Fire cosmology already credited to him unnamed in this roster.
Intra-tradition: his fragments (DK 44) are the direct source of the Limit/Unlimited ontology.
Intra-tradition: the verse catechism formalizes and outlives the oral maxims it descends from.
Intra-tradition: the akousmatikoi faction is defined by exactly this body of oral maxims.
Intra-tradition: the mathematikoi faction's demonstrative science is the branch that produced the Central Fire cosmology.
Intra-tradition: reputed wife/disciple and emblem of the school's documented women members.
Intra-tradition: his legendary punishment for disclosure dramatizes the tension between the secrecy-keeping and knowledge-publishing wings of the school.

The identical ten-point triangular diagram in two registers: the Pythagorean brotherhood's supreme oath-object and cosmological seed, abstracted by later esoteric divination into a numerological schema and method.

dossier.essence: A philosophical-religious movement founded by Pythagoras of Samos (c. 570–495 BCE) at Croton in Magna Graecia around 530 BCE, combining a mathematical account of the cosmos with a way of life aimed at purifying the immortal, transmigrating soul. Its adherents formed a secretive, ascetic brotherhood bound by ritual rules, later remembered as split between the rule-keeping akousmatikoi and the demonstrating mathematikoi. Its central claim is that number and ratio are the principle and structure of all things.
dossier.cosmos: All things are number or are ordered by number: the same ratios that produce concordant musical intervals are held to govern the heavens (the 'harmony of the spheres'). The soul is immortal and divine, fallen into a cycle of reincarnation (metempsychosis) that migrates it across human, animal, and even plant bodies according to conduct, so that philosophy and purity aim at eventual release. Philolaus of Croton (5th c. BCE) taught a cosmos of ten bodies revolving around a central fire, displacing the Earth from the center for reasons of numerical perfection. Good and evil are mapped onto a table of opposites — limit against the unlimited, odd against even.
dossier.practice: An ascetic communal life governed by the akousmata — cryptic oral precepts and taboos (famously the prohibition on beans, and rules of silence, diet, and ritual conduct). Purification was pursued through mathematics, music, self-examination, and, in much of the tradition, vegetarianism, since a killed animal might house a human soul. The Crotonian brotherhood also held political power until a violent backlash burned its meeting-houses in the mid-5th century BCE.
dossier.canon: No scripture — the early teaching was oral and secret, reconstructed from fragments of Philolaus and Archytas and from Aristotle's reports (including his lost 'On the Pythagoreans'). The detailed biographies are much later and unreliable: Diogenes Laertius, Porphyry's 'Life of Pythagoras', and Iamblichus's 'On the Pythagorean Life' (3rd–4th c. CE).
dossier.reflects: The sacralization of number — the impulse to locate the divine order and the soul's salvation in mathematics, ratio, and demonstrable structure rather than in myth or sacrifice.
sources: Walter Burkert, 'Lore and Science in Ancient Pythagoreanism' (1972)
Carl A. Huffman, 'Philolaus of Croton: Pythagorean and Presocratic' (1993)
Christoph Riedweg, 'Pythagoras: His Life, Teaching, and Influence' (2005)
